content.full_text:
Re:
Farn PolrrrcAI- PRACTTcES C oMMISSIoN
P.O. Box 807 o 428 J Street . Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
January 3T,2002
James R. Sutton
Nielsen, Merksamer, Parrinello
Mueller & Naylor, LLP
591 Redwood Highway, #4000
Mill Valley, California 94941
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-02-001
Dear Mr. Sutton:
This letter is in response to your question regarding the continuing vitality of
Government Code $ 84106.5, published as part of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")1
in Deerings' Official Codes of California. Because your question is general in nature and
does not seek advice on a particular matter, we provide you with informal assistance.2
QUESTION
Is Government Code $ 84106.5, included in the version of the Act printed in
Deerings' Official Codes of California, ar enforceable provision of the Act even though
it is not included in the Act as pubiished by the FPPC? If S 84106.5 is an enforceable
part of the Act, why is it not included in the Act as published by the FPPC, and are other
enforceable provisions also not included in the Commission's version of the Act?
CONCLUSION
Section 84106.5 is not an enforceable provision of the Act. The California
Supreme Court expressly so ruled in 1990 and forthatreason, since 1991, S 84106.5
has not been printed in the Act as published by the FPPC.
I Govemment Code $$ 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, $$ 18109-18997, of the
Califorrua Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide flre requestor r,vith the imnruruty provided by a Commission opinion
or formal r,vritten advice. (See enclosed, Regulation 18329(c).)
@


File No. I-02-001
Page No. 2
FACTS
Section 84106.5 was originally adopted (as $ 84106) as parl of Proposition 68 on
the June 1988 ballot. Although most of Proposition 68 was "trumped" by PropositionT3,
which received more votes atthe same election, the FPPC determined that $ 84106.5
nevertheless took effect because it did not conflict with any provision of PropositionT3.
The Legislature later amended and renumbered $ 84106 as $ 84106.5. (Stats. 1990, Ch.
655, I Z.) 3 As renumbered, the statute is currently included in Deerings' Offrcial Codes
of California, but is not printed in the version of the Act published by the FPPC.
ANALYSIS
Your recital of the crucial facts is correct through 1988. On November 9, 1988,
the FPPC released its opinion In the Matter of Bell, et al. (11 FPPC Ops. 1.) In that
opinion the Commission addressed the question "[w]hat, if any, of Proposition 68...
survives the passage of PropositionT3 .. . by a greater number of votes?" To answer this
question, the Commission construed art.II, $ 10(b) of the California Constitution, which
states: "If provisions of two or more measures approved at the same election conflict,
those of the measure receiving the highest affirmative vote shall prevail."
The Commission read this constitutional provision to permit usage of settled rules
of statutory construction to preserve those portions of Proposition 68 which were not in
conflict with Proposition 73, and which in addition were severable from those portions
of Proposition 68 which did conflict with Proposition 73. Pursuing this analysis the
Commission found that a number of provisions from Proposition 68 could be integrated
into the Act, including specifically $ 84106. (Bell, supra atpp 2 and24.)
However, the matter wound up in court. As pertinent here, the sponsor of
Proposition 68, Taxpayers to Limit Campaign Spending, sought a writ of mandate, in
effect asking the Second District Court of Appeal to order the Commission to add more
of Proposition 68 to the Act. Like the Commission, the Court of Appeal read the
constitutional provision as requiring that the court attempt to reconcile and give effect to
both measures to the extent possible. But the Court of Appeal read the Constitution more
broadly than had the Commission, and as a result it ordered the Commission to enforce
numerous additional provisions of Proposition 68.
3 Approved by the Govemor on Septernber 8, 1990, filed with the Secretary of State September 11, 1990.
Notrng that $ 84106 was added by Proposition 68, the bill renurnbering flre section arnended the statute
only insofar as it was renunrbered $ 84106.5; the Act had already contained a $ 84106, r.vhich had not been
repealed either by Proposition 68 or by Proposition 73. The renumberirg ofthis statute, distinguishing it
fronr the other $ 84106, r,vas simply a step in integrating this provision of Proposition 68 into the Act,
There r,vas no substantive amendment of this statute, and the Legislature's renunrbering did not alter the
fact that the prorzision r,vas ilserted into flre Act by Proposition 68. As r,vill appear below, this origin is
dispositive on the question of its current enforceability.


File No. I-02-001
Page No. 3
The Commission appealed this decision to the California Supreme Court, which
entered its opinion on November l, 1990. (Tmpayers to Limit Campaign Spending v.
FPPC (1990) 51Cal.3d 7a4.) The SupremeCourtnotedthatthequestionbeforeitwas
one of first impression (id. at747), and concluded that both the Commission and the
Court of Appeal had wrongly construed art. II, $ 10(b) to permit the integration of parts
of Proposition 68 into the Act as amended by PropositionT3. The Supreme Court's
position is succinctly summarized as follows:
"Does section 10(b) contemplate enforcement of any
provisions of an initiative that receives a majority of the
votes cast when another initiative on the same ballot,
directed to the same subject and offered as a competing
regulatory scheme, receives a greater majority? We
conclude thatit does not." (Taxpayers, supra> 51 Cal.3d at
76s.)
Following the logic of the Bell Opinion, supra, the Commission had included
$ 84106 in its published versions of the Act for the years 1989 and 1990. After the
Supreme Court's decision in Taxpayers, it was clear that S 84106 was not an enforceable
provision of the Act. Because it was not an enforceable provision, the Commission did
not include $ 84106 (renumbered in 1990 as $ 84106 5) in its versions of the Act
published from l99l to the present. We trust that this answers your question as to the
status of $ 84106.5, and your ancillary questions as well.
If you wish to discuss this matter furtheq please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General CounselturM
By: Lawrence T. Woodlock
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
LTW:mf
I.\Adviceltrs\O2-001
Enclosure
